Transport Layer

Transport Layer — Real-World Parallel

Apartment Analogy

  • Building address brings packages to the right building
  • Reception checks flat number and delivers to family
  • Without reception, packages pile up undelivered
  • Transport Layer is the reception for your computer

Transport Layer Analogy Mapped

Your Computer

  • Computer = apartment building; apps are the families inside
  • Data packets = packages arriving from the internet
  • Transport Layer = reception, routing packets to correct app
  • Port number = flat number; IP address = building address

Transport Layer Core Role

End-to-End Communication

  • Connects application processes on two different host devices
  • IP address delivers data to the correct device
  • Port numbers direct packets to the right application
  • Transport Layer runs only on end hosts, not routers

Transport Layer in Network Stack

Network Stack Layers

  • Data Link Layer: node-to-node, directly connected devices
  • Network Layer: host-to-host routing across networks
  • Transport Layer: process-to-process across different hosts
  • Transport Layer runs only on end devices

Transport Layer sender steps

Sender Processing

  • Receive message from the Application Layer
  • Break large message into smaller segments
  • Add header: source port and destination port
  • Pass segments down to the Network Layer

Transport Layer receiving data steps

Receiver Processing

  • Network Layer delivers incoming segments to Transport Layer
  • Transport Layer reassembles segments into original message
  • Port number in header identifies the target application
  • Data delivered to the correct application process

Transport Layer Core Operations

Key Functions

  • Segmentation: splits large data into smaller network segments
  • Process-to-process delivery: port numbers route data to correct app
  • Multiplexing/Demultiplexing: multiple apps share one network interface
  • Error detection: checksums identify corruption; TCP ensures reliability

Core responsibilities at a glance

Key Takeaways

  • Segments large application data into smaller, manageable units
  • Delivers data to correct app via port numbers
  • Multiplexes and demultiplexes traffic across multiple applications
  • TCP adds reliability, flow control, and congestion control

Process-to-Process Delivery

Transport Layer Delivery Mechanism

Process-to-Process

  • IP address identifies the destination computer or device
  • Port number identifies the target application or process
  • Transport Layer routes data to the correct process
  • Together they enable process-to-process delivery

Transport Layer process addressing

Port Numbers

  • 16-bit unsigned integer; range 0 to 65,535
  • Uniquely identifies a process or application on a host
  • IANA divides ports into three distinct ranges
  • Well-Known, Registered, and Dynamic/Ephemeral categories
Well-Known Ports0 – 1,023
HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS
Registered Ports1,024 – 49,151
MySQL, PostgreSQL, RDP
Dynamic / Ephemeral49,152 – 65,535
Temporary client ports

IANA Reserved, Ports 0–1023

Well-Known Ports

  • Ports 0–1023 reserved by IANA for standard protocols
Protocol
Port
Purpose
HTTP / HTTPS
80 / 443
Web Traffic (Plain & Secure)
FTP / SSH
21 / 22
File Transfer & Remote Access
DNS / SMTP
53 / 25
Name Resolution & Email

Port ranges and OS assignment

Registered & Dynamic

  • Registered Ports: 1024–49151, used by software vendors
  • Dynamic Ports: 49152–65535, assigned by OS temporarily
  • Released automatically when the connection closes
  • Ephemeral range varies slightly across operating systems

Hotel Analogy for Ports

Port Ranges Analogy

  • Hotel address = IP address; room number = port number
  • Well-Known Ports (0–1023): fixed spots — reception, gym, restaurant
  • Registered Ports (1024–49151): conference rooms booked by companies
  • Dynamic Ports (49152–65535): temporary rooms for walk-in guests

IP Address Plus Port Number

Socket Addresses

  • Socket address = IP address + port number
  • IP address identifies the computer on the network
  • Port number identifies the specific application
  • Format: 192.168.1.10:80 uniquely locates one app

Identifying Network Connections Uniquely

The 4-Tuple

  • Source IP + Source Port identify the sender's socket
  • Destination IP + Destination Port identify the receiver's socket
  • 4-tuple uniquely identifies every active network connection
  • IP = building, Port = office, Socket = full address

Client-Server Architecture

Roles, Sockets, Communication Flow

Client-Server Model

  • Client initiates contact; server waits to be contacted
  • Both roles communicate through sockets
  • Socket: doorway between application and Transport Layer
  • Example: web browser (client) → Google web server

Client-Server Architecture Step 1

Server Listens

  • Server binds to a well-known port (e.g., 443)
  • Opens a socket to receive connections
  • Enters listening state — ready for clients
  • Waits passively for incoming client requests

Ephemeral Port, Socket Setup

Client Connects

  • OS assigns client a dynamic port (e.g., 52431)
  • Client opens socket: Client IP + port 52431
  • Both sides now hold a complete socket address
  • Client targets Server IP:443 to initiate connection

Client-Server Communication Flow

Request & Response

  • Client sends request: Your IP:52431 → Google IP:443
  • Server processes request and prepares a response
  • Response reverses direction: Google IP:443 → Your IP:52431
  • Source and destination ports swap in every response

Client-Server Port and Socket Behavior

Communication Observations

  • Server always listens on a fixed, well-known port
  • Client gets a temporary ephemeral port assigned by the OS
  • Both processes communicate through their respective sockets
  • Socket = full delivery address: IP address + port

Multiplexing & Demultiplexing

Combining Multiple App Streams

Sender Multiplexing

  • Multiple apps send data simultaneously via sockets
  • Transport Layer adds source and destination port headers
  • Data wrapped into segments, passed to lower layers
  • All app streams merge into one outgoing network flow

Receiver-Side Segment Delivery

Receiver Demultiplexing

  • Segments arrive as a single incoming flow from network
  • Transport Layer reads destination port from each segment header
  • Each segment delivered to the correct application socket
  • Like reception routing parcels by flat number to families
Network
Incoming Segment Flow
Transport Layer
Reads Destination Port
Socket A
Port 3000
Socket B
Port 8080
Socket C
Port 5432
App 1
App 2
App 3

Transport Layer Port Handling

Mux vs Demux

  • Two sides of the same Transport Layer coin.
Aspect
Multiplexing
Demultiplexing
Side
Sender
Receiver
Direction
Many apps → one flow
One flow → many apps
Port Usage
Port numbers added
Destination port read

Socket Identification at Transport Layer

TCP vs UDP

  • TCP uses 4-tuple; UDP uses 2-tuple for sockets
Protocol
Socket ID Tuple
Shared/Unique Socket
Example
UDP
Dest IP + Dest Port
Shared — one socket per port
DNS port 53
TCP
Src + Dst IP & Port (4-tuple)
Unique — per connection
HTTPS port 443

Transport Services

No setup, send immediately

Connectionless Service

  • No connection setup — data sent immediately
  • Each message is independent, no ordering guarantee
  • No delivery confirmation — packets may be lost
  • Used in DNS, gaming, video calls, streaming

TCP reliable delivery phases

Connection-Oriented Service

  • Connection established before any data is transferred
  • Data arrives in order — no duplication or loss
  • Lost segments retransmitted automatically by TCP
  • Formal termination closes connection after communication

Connectionless vs Connection-Oriented

Service Comparison

  • Trade-offs determine which service fits each application.
Feature
Connectionless
Connection-Oriented
Setup & Overhead
None · Low
Required · Higher
Reliability & Order
No guarantee
Reliable · In-order
Best For
Video, gaming, DNS
Web, email, file transfer

Application Requirements Drive Choice

Choosing a Service

  • Neither service is inherently better than the other
  • Speed priority? Choose connectionless service (UDP)
  • Reliability priority? Choose connection-oriented (TCP)
  • Application needs determine the right transport service

Transport Layer Core Concepts

Key Takeaways

  • Transport Layer enables end-to-end, process-to-process communication
  • IP identifies the host; port number identifies the application
  • Socket address (IP:Port) is the complete delivery address
  • UDP = connectionless & fast; TCP = connection-oriented & reliable

Transport Layer Concepts Check

Quiz

  • 1.What does a port number identify on a host?
  • 2.What two components form a socket address?
  • 3.Which protocol provides reliable, ordered delivery?
  • 4.What port range is reserved for well-known protocols?

Thank you!
